description:
You are acting as an internal compliance-officer who has just received two raw
    CSV extracts from the infrastructure-scanning platform.  
    Your goal is to correlate the data, identify every host that currently has at
    least one vulnerability whose “severity” field is equal to the string
    “Critical”, and then produce a pair of artefacts that will be handed over to
    senior management:

    1. A CSV report called
       /home/user/audit_output/critical_hosts_report.csv  
    2. A JSON one-page summary called
       /home/user/audit_output/summary.json  

    A very small demonstration dataset has already been staged for you under
    /home/user/audit_data/ :

        /home/user/audit_data/hosts.csv
        /home/user/audit_data/vulnerabilities.csv

    Their exact, complete contents are:

        ── hosts.csv ──
        host_id,hostname,ip,owner
        1,db-prod-01,10.0.0.11,finance
        2,web-prod-02,10.0.0.21,marketing
        3,db-test-01,10.0.1.11,finance
        4,ci-server,10.0.2.5,devops

        ── vulnerabilities.csv ──
        host_id,cve_id,severity,fix_available
        1,CVE-2022-0001,Critical,Yes
        1,CVE-2022-0002,High,Yes
        2,CVE-2021-1111,Critical,No
        3,CVE-2020-2222,Medium,Yes
        4,CVE-2019-3333,Low,No

    Tasks you must complete (≈ 5-10 commands in total is expected, but the exact
    steps are up to you):

    A. Create the directory /home/user/audit_output/  if it is not already
       present.  All deliverables must live inside this directory.

    B. Produce the file /home/user/audit_output/critical_hosts_report.csv with 
       the following **exact** column order and header line
       (spelling and character case are significant and will be verified
       automatically):

           hostname,ip,cve_id,fix_available

       • One line per individual “Critical” vulnerability.  
       • Rows must be sorted by hostname in ascending
         (lexicographical) order.  
       • Use the data supplied above—do not invent or remove rows.  
       • The final file, once finished, **must** therefore look
         character-for-character exactly like this (including the final
         newline at the end of the last line):

           hostname,ip,cve_id,fix_available
           db-prod-01,10.0.0.11,CVE-2022-0001,Yes
           web-prod-02,10.0.0.21,CVE-2021-1111,No

    C. Create /home/user/audit_output/summary.json  containing the following
       seven key/value pairs **in exactly the order shown** so that automated
       diff-based testing succeeds.  All numeric values are integers,
       not strings, and the JSON must be completely minified—i.e. no
       pretty-printing/line-breaks:

       {
         "total_hosts_scanned":4,
         "total_vulnerabilities":5,
         "critical_vulnerabilities":2,
         "high_vulnerabilities":1,
         "medium_vulnerabilities":1,
         "low_vulnerabilities":1,
         "hosts_with_critical_findings":2,
         "report_file":"/home/user/audit_output/critical_hosts_report.csv"
       }

       IMPORTANT formatting requirements that will be checked byte-for-byte:
         • Exactly the field order above  
         • No spaces other than the single one after each colon shown above  
         • JSON opens with “{” and closes with “}” on the same line  

    D. For auditing purposes, also create the plain-text file
       /home/user/audit_output/processed_files.log  listing every source file you
       touched, one absolute path per line, in the following required order:

         /home/user/audit_data/hosts.csv
         /home/user/audit_data/vulnerabilities.csv

       The file must end with a trailing newline.

    Verification guidance for you:
       • All three artefacts must be present with the **exact** contents and
         paths described above; automated tests will read them as literal
         strings.  
       • The exercise is intentionally small, so any standard CLI tooling
         (awk, csvkit, jq, python -c one-liners, etc.) is acceptable—choose what
         you find simplest.  
       • Remember to make the audit_output directory before writing files, and
         do not move or rename the original dataset.

    When you’re satisfied that the generated files match the specifications
    above, you’ve completed the task.
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the agent starts:
      Directory: /home/user/audit_data/
        - hosts.csv               (contents exactly as listed in the public
                                   description)
        - vulnerabilities.csv     (contents exactly as listed)

    Items that **must** exist after correct completion:

      1. /home/user/audit_output/critical_hosts_report.csv
         Expected exact contents (including trailing newline):

           hostname,ip,cve_id,fix_available
           db-prod-01,10.0.0.11,CVE-2022-0001,Yes
           web-prod-02,10.0.0.21,CVE-2021-1111,No

      2. /home/user/audit_output/summary.json
         Expected exact contents (single-line, minified, trailing newline not
         required but allowed):

           {"total_hosts_scanned":4,"total_vulnerabilities":5,"critical_vulnerabilities":2,"high_vulnerabilities":1,"medium_vulnerabilities":1,"low_vulnerabilities":1,"hosts_with_critical_findings":2,"report_file":"/home/user/audit_output/critical_hosts_report.csv"}

      3. /home/user/audit_output/processed_files.log
         Expected exact contents (including trailing newline):

           /home/user/audit_data/hosts.csv
           /home/user/audit_data/vulnerabilities.csv

    Permissions: default user-writable files (mode 0644) are sufficient; audit_output
    directory should be 0755 or similar so that the agent can create files.
